subject: Basement flooded again on Eastern Ave - 3rd time in 18 months
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo,

I am writing because I am at the end of my rope. Sometime between 11 PM Tuesday and 6 AM Wednesday this week my finished basement at 1842 Eastern Ave took on roughly 14 inches of water. This is the third time since the fall of 2024 that I have had water in this basement, and the second time this year alone. I lost a furnace last time, and this time it looks like the water heater, the washer and dryer, and most of what was in storage down there.

When the rain comes hard the storm drain at the corner of Eastern and 19th simply stops taking water. I have stood at my front window and watched it bubble UP out of the grate instead of going down. Water then runs back down my driveway, which slopes toward the house, and comes in through the foundation wall on the north side and up through the floor drain. My sump pump cannot keep up - it ran continuously and the discharge just pooled in the yard and came right back.

My neighbors at 1838 and 1850 had the same thing happen the same night. We have lived through this enough times now to know it is not just bad luck. There is something wrong with the storm sewer capacity in these blocks and it has gotten worse, not better. I have receipts and photos from all three events and I am happy to share them.

I need to know what the Village is going to do. I cannot keep absorbing thousands of dollars in damage every time we get a heavy rain. Please tell me who I should be talking to.

Marcus Bilski

subject: Eastern Ave & 19th flooding - coordinating a Village response
body_clean:
Mr. Bilski,

Thank you for the detailed account, and I am genuinely sorry about the damage. What you describe - the grate at Eastern and 19th surcharging and pushing water back up out of the storm sewer - is exactly the kind of thing my staff needs to document right now while it is fresh, so I am copying the people who can actually move on this.

Frank DiMeo runs Public Works and can get a crew out to inspect and jet the catch basins on Eastern between 18th and 20th. Tom Reyes is our village engineer and has been looking at storm-sewer capacity in the low-lying blocks around Eastern and 19th for some time; I want him to fold your event into that analysis. Lorena, please open a drainage complaint file under Mr. Bilski's address and pull any prior reports from this block since fall 2024 so we have the full history in one place.

Mr. Bilski, please do send the photos and receipts from all three events - dates, depths, and any timestamps you have. That documentation matters not just for our records but potentially for a hazard-mitigation grant application, which Tom can speak to.

I also want to be straight with you: if the underlying problem is undersized storm sewer, the real fix is a capital project, and those take time and money. But pumping, catch-basin cleaning, and getting the system flowing at full capacity are things we can act on immediately, and we will. You will hear from Frank's office within 48 hours.

Daniel R. Okonkwo
Mayor, Village of Bellwood

subject: Field findings - Eastern Ave catch basins and 19th Ave outfall
body_clean:
Mayor, Tom, all,

My crew was on Eastern Ave Thursday and Friday. Here is what we found, and it is not great.

We vactored five catch basins on Eastern between 18th and 20th. Three of them were 60 to 80 percent full of sediment, gravel, and root mass - the one at the Eastern/19th corner that Mr. Bilski described was the worst, essentially choked. We pulled out close to two yards of material between the five. That alone was reducing inlet capacity badly, and we have it flowing better now. But cleaning the basins does not change the fact that the 12-inch storm main running north under 19th is undersized for these blocks. When we ran a quick dye and camera check, the line was already near capacity at normal flow.

The bigger issue is downstream. The 19th Ave storm sewer ties into a culvert that crosses under Mannheim Rd, and Mannheim is a state route - that culvert is IDOT's. If that culvert is restricted or backing up during a storm, no amount of work we do upstream will keep these basements dry, because the water has nowhere to go. We need IDOT to tell us the condition and capacity of that culvert.

I rescheduled crews to camera the full run on 19th from Eastern to Mannheim next week. In the meantime I recommend we stage portable pumps before the next forecasted heavy rain rather than chase it after. We own two 4-inch trash pumps; for an event like Tuesday's we would want more than that.

Frank DiMeo
Director of Public Works

subject: Storm-sewer capacity analysis - Eastern/19th basin and proposed relief project
body_clean:
Mayor, Frank, all,

Frank's field findings line up with the hydraulic picture I have been building, so let me lay out the engineering and where I think this needs to go.

The area draining to the Eastern/19th system is about 38 acres of mostly older residential lots with high impervious cover and minimal on-site detention - these blocks were platted long before detention was required. The existing trunk is a 12-inch reinforced concrete pipe that then steps to 15-inch before the Mannheim culvert. Running the numbers, that system was designed for roughly a 2-year storm. The May 12 event that flooded Mr. Bilski measured 3.9 inches in about six hours at our Public Works gauge, which is close to a 25-year storm. The system is simply overwhelmed, and surcharging at the Eastern/19th grate is the predictable result.

What I have been scoping is a storm-sewer relief project: upsize the 19th Ave trunk from 12-inch to 36-inch RCP from Eastern Ave to the Mannheim culvert, add inlet capacity at the three worst corners, and construct an underground detention vault under the 19th Ave parkway to buffer peak flows. My planning-level estimate is 1.4 million dollars, design and construction. That would bring the level of protection up to roughly a 10-year storm for these blocks, which is the realistic target for a retrofit in a built-out area.

The good news: this is a textbook candidate for the FEMA Building Resilient Infrastructure and Communities (BRIC) program, administered in Illinois through IEMA, which can cover up to 75 percent federal share. With documented repetitive flood damage - exactly what Mr. Bilski and his neighbors have - we have a strong benefit-cost story. I would like authorization to develop the application; the next IEMA sub-application window is mid-September.

None of this helps anyone this storm season, so I fully support Frank staging pumps. But I want the long-term fix on the table now.

Tom Reyes, P.E.
Village Engineer

subject: 1907 19th Ave - sewage backup, not just stormwater
body_clean:
Dear Mayor Okonkwo,

I understand you are already aware of the flooding around Eastern and 19th, but I want to make sure my situation is on the record because mine is worse than rainwater. At 1907 19th Ave, when the storm hit on May 12, what came up through my basement floor drain was not clean water - it was sewage. There is no mistaking the smell. I have a combined situation where the sanitary and storm appear to be interacting, and during the storm the line backed up into my house.

This is a health issue, not just a property issue. I have two grandchildren who stay with me on weekends and I will not let them downstairs until this is professionally remediated, which I am told will run several thousand dollars. I have already paid 1,100 dollars just to have it pumped and sanitized once.

I have lived at this address for 31 years. The flooding on this block was never like this until the last couple of years. Something has changed and I want to understand what. Is the Village's sewer system the cause, and if so, what is my recourse? I have heard neighbors talk about the Village having some responsibility when the public main is the source of a backup. I am not trying to be litigious, I am trying to protect my home and my health.

Please have someone who actually understands the sewer system contact me directly. I can be reached during the day.

Denise Karpinski

subject: Health response for sewage backups - 19th Ave block
body_clean:
Mayor, Tom, Frank,

Mrs. Karpinski's report changes the risk profile here, and I want to flag it clearly. A sewage backup is a Category 3 contaminated-water event. Anyone with that exposure needs to keep children and pets out of the affected area until it is professionally cleaned and disinfected, and porous materials - carpet, drywall, upholstery - generally have to be removed, not just dried. I will call her today and walk her through remediation standards and give her our list of licensed restoration contractors.

More importantly, if sanitary sewage is surcharging into basements during storms, that tells us stormwater is getting into the sanitary system somewhere - infiltration and inflow, cross-connections, or surcharge from a combined segment. That is a public-health concern across the whole block, not one house. I would like Frank's crew, when they camera the storm line, to also check whether there is any cross-connection between the storm and sanitary on this run.

For any resident reporting a sewage backup, please route them to me in addition to the drainage file. I want to track exposures. I would also suggest the Village remind affected residents in writing that they should photograph everything and contact their insurers promptly, since some homeowner policies require notice within a short window for water and backup claims.

If we get another heavy rain before remediation, the health exposure compounds. This raises the urgency on the pumping plan considerably.

Priya Nair
Village Health Officer

subject: Mannheim Rd culvert at 19th Ave - jurisdiction and inspection
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo and Mr. Reyes,

Thank you for your letter and the follow-up call regarding the cross culvert under Mannheim Rd (IL Route 72 corridor) near the 19th Ave alignment. You are correct that Mannheim is a state-maintained route and that the cross-drainage structure beneath it falls under IDOT District 1 jurisdiction.

I have pulled our structure records. The culvert in question is a 48-inch corrugated metal pipe installed in 1971. Our most recent inventory inspection rated the invert as fair with measurable section loss, and there is a noted history of debris accumulation at the inlet headwall. I will be candid that a 1971 CMP at this age may be a contributing constriction during high flows, and I am scheduling a field inspection within the next three weeks to assess current condition, silt level, and any blockage. I will share the report.

Two things I need to manage expectations on. First, IDOT's responsibility is the cross-drainage structure conveying flow under our route; the municipal storm sewer connecting to it remains the Village's. If the bottleneck is partly the connection geometry or the local system, that is a shared design conversation, not solely a state fix. Second, any improvement to a state structure goes through our programming process and is not quick. If the Village pursues a relief project that requires modifying or replacing the culvert, you will need an IDOT permit for work in state right-of-way, and we should coordinate the hydraulics early so your design and our structure are sized consistently.

I am willing to be a constructive partner here. Please send me Mr. Reyes's hydraulic analysis when it is ready so my hydraulics unit can review it against our culvert capacity.

Raymond Sczepanski, IDOT District 1

subject: 1855 Eastern Ave - flooded a SECOND time June 8, want answers
body_clean:
Mayor,

I was told by my neighbor Marcus Bilski that there is supposedly a whole effort underway about the flooding on our blocks. Well, it flooded AGAIN on Sunday June 8. My basement at 1855 Eastern Ave took about eight inches. So whatever is being studied and analyzed and coordinated, it is not stopping the water from coming into our homes, and I am losing patience.

I watched a Public Works truck cleaning the drains a few weeks ago and I appreciate that, but the corner at 19th still backed up on Sunday just like before. I do not want to hear about a grant application that might happen in September and a project that might happen in two or three years. That does nothing for me when the next storm hits in July. What is the plan for THIS summer? Are you putting pumps out there or not? Because everyone on this block heard there were going to be pumps staged before the next big rain, and on June 8 there were no pumps.

I also want to know, in plain language, whether the Village considers this its fault. We pay our taxes. The storm sewer is the Village's. If you knew the system was undersized - and from what Mr. Bilski forwarded me, your own engineer is saying it was built for a 2-year storm - then why are residents eating the cost of the damage?

I would like a written answer, and I would like to know when the next public meeting on this is, because I guarantee I am not the only one on these blocks who is going to show up.

Anthony Vega

subject: Emergency pumping quote - Eastern/19th, per your RFQ
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo, Mr. DiMeo, Mr. Reyes,

Per your request following the June 8 event, Verdi Excavating is pleased to provide a quote for emergency standby and active dewatering services for the Eastern Ave and 19th Ave low-lying blocks through the remainder of this storm season.

We propose the following. Mobilization and staging of two 6-inch diesel trash pumps (1,400 GPM each) plus one 4-inch backup, with discharge hose routed to the 19th Ave system downstream of the constriction: 4,800 dollars mobilization. Standby retainer, on-call within two hours of a flood watch, including fuel and one operator: 2,200 dollars per month. Active operation during an event, two-operator crew: 185 dollars per hour with a four-hour minimum per call-out. We can also provide vac-truck support for catch-basin cleaning at 295 dollars per hour if your crews are stretched.

For a season commitment through September 30 - which I understand aligns with your grant timeline - we would estimate roughly 18,000 to 26,000 dollars total depending on the number and severity of call-outs. If the Village prefers, we can structure this as standby plus actuals rather than a flat seasonal number.

I will note from our own experience working in those blocks that the real choke point is downstream at the Mannheim culvert; pumping will relieve the worst of the basement flooding but cannot fully fix a backwater condition coming off a restricted state structure. We are glad to coordinate our discharge points with Mr. Reyes so we are not just pushing water into an already-full line.

Quote valid 30 days. We can mobilize within 72 hours of a notice to proceed, which matters with July weather coming.

Verdi Excavating

subject: Funding the emergency pumping and the local match question
body_clean:
Mayor, all,

Let me put numbers around the two decisions on the table so the Board has a clean picture.

First, the emergency pumping. The Verdi quote lands in the 18,000 to 26,000 dollar range for the season. I can cover this from the Sewer Fund's contingency line, which has about 41,000 dollars uncommitted for FY26, without a budget amendment. Given the health exposure Priya flagged and a second flood event on June 8, I am comfortable recommending we authorize a standby-plus-actuals agreement rather than the flat number - we only pay the 2,200 monthly retainer and pay the hourly rate when pumps actually run, which protects us if July is dry. This can go on the consent agenda or, given the emergency, be approved by the Village Manager under the emergency-procurement provision of our purchasing ordinance up to her threshold, with ratification at the next meeting.

Second, and bigger, the 1.4 million relief project. If the BRIC application succeeds at 75 percent federal share, the local match is roughly 350,000 dollars. That is not contingency money - it is a capital commitment we would need to plan for over the FY27-28 capital program, likely through the Sewer Fund and possibly a portion of home-rule sales tax or a bond. I do not want anyone, including residents, to think the grant means the project is free; the match is real and we should be honest about it publicly.

One caution: before the Village makes any statement about fault or responsibility for damages, I would defer to the Village Attorney. There is a meaningful difference between committing to fix infrastructure and accepting liability for past damages, and the Illinois Tort Immunity Act treatment of drainage and discretionary decisions is something counsel needs to weigh in on before we put anything in writing to Mr. Vega or Mrs. Karpinski.

David Okafor
Finance Director

subject: Cook County stormwater - MWRD coordination and possible cost-share
body_clean:
Mayor Okonkwo and Mr. Reyes,

Thank you for looping the County into the Eastern/19th flooding discussion. A few items that may help.

First, stormwater management authority in Cook County rests with the Metropolitan Water Reclamation District (MWRD), and your proposed relief project will need to comply with the Cook County Watershed Management Ordinance, particularly the detention and release-rate requirements. The underground detention vault Mr. Reyes described is exactly the kind of feature that will be reviewed, so engage MWRD's permitting staff early - I can make an introduction to the right reviewer for the Addison Creek watershed, which is the relevant drainage area for your blocks.

Second, you should know the Addison Creek watershed has a long-documented flooding history, and MWRD has both completed and planned projects in this corridor. It is worth checking whether your Eastern/19th system ties into anything MWRD is already programming - there may be an opportunity to coordinate or even cost-share rather than building in isolation.

Third, on the FEMA/IEMA hazard-mitigation angle: Cook County maintains a Multi-Hazard Mitigation Plan, and for your project to be eligible for FEMA mitigation funding, the hazard and the jurisdiction generally need to be reflected in that plan. The good news is Bellwood is a participating jurisdiction. I would confirm your repetitive-loss properties on Eastern and 19th are captured in the plan's data, because that strengthens both eligibility and your benefit-cost analysis. Mr. Reyes, if you send me your damage documentation, I can check it against the County's repetitive-loss records.

Happy to set up a joint call with County stormwater staff and MWRD. This is solvable, but it works best when the Village, County, MWRD, and IDOT are aligned on the hydraulics before anyone designs anything.

Gwendolyn Achebe, Cook County

subject: Eastern/19th flooding - where we are and what happens next
body_clean:
Team, and to the residents I am also writing separately,

I want to consolidate where this stands, because a lot has moved in a few weeks and I want everyone pulling in the same direction.

Immediate, this season: Karen, please execute the emergency standby pumping agreement with Verdi on the standby-plus-actuals structure David recommended, under the emergency-procurement provision, with ratification at the next Board meeting. I want pumps able to mobilize within 72 hours and staged ahead of the next flood watch - not chased afterward. The residents on these blocks were promised pumps and on June 8 there were none. That cannot happen again. Frank, finish cameraing the 19th Ave run, including the cross-connection check Priya asked for, and get me a date.

Near term: Tom, you are authorized to develop the BRIC sub-application for the September IEMA window. Coordinate the hydraulics with Mr. Sczepanski at IDOT on the 1971 Mannheim culvert and with Ms. Achebe on MWRD and the Addison Creek watershed before we finalize any design - I do not want us sizing a 36-inch pipe into a 48-inch culvert that is the real bottleneck. Confirm our Eastern and 19th repetitive-loss properties are captured in the County mitigation plan. David, begin scoping how we carry the roughly 350,000 dollar local match in the FY27-28 capital program.

On liability: nobody from the Village puts anything in writing to residents about fault until the Village Attorney has reviewed it. We will be transparent and we will fix the infrastructure, but David is right that committing to a fix and accepting damages liability are different things, and counsel decides the language.

Priya, keep tracking the sewage-exposure cases and make sure Mrs. Karpinski has what she needs.

Finally, I am scheduling a public information meeting at Village Hall, 3200 Washington Blvd, for residents of the Eastern and 19th corridor. Mr. Vega, Mr. Bilski, Mrs. Karpinski - you will all be invited and I want you there. Tina will get a date out this week. We are not going to make this go away with one storm, but I want these neighbors to see exactly who is doing what and on what timeline.

Daniel R. Okonkwo
Mayor, Village of Bellwood
